description: FPV expert knowledge rules. Each rule encodes real-world FPV knowledge for build validation, swap fairness, and beginner guidance.

severity_levels.hard_constraint: Physically impossible or dangerous. Must block.
severity_levels.strong_recommendation: Will work badly. Should warn prominently.

tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: 65mm whoops need 0702 or 0802 motors. Anything larger won't fit the frame ducts.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: 65mm duct diameter constrains motor stator to 07xx-08xx class. Larger stators exceed motor mount spacing.

tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: 75mm whoops use 0802, 1002, or 1102 motors. 0802 for gentle 1S, 1102 for punchy 2S.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: 75mm duct fits 08xx-11xx stators. 0802 is standard 1S, 1002 bridges 1S-2S, 1102 is 2S optimised.

tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: 65mm whoops only fit 31mm props. They're the only size that clears the ducts.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: 65mm duct inner diameter constrains prop to 31mm class. Gemfan 31mm 3-blade is community standard.

tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: 75mm whoops use 40mm props. Tri-blade is standard, quad-blade gives more grip but draws more current.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: 75mm duct fits 40mm prop class. Tri-blade balances thrust and efficiency. Quad-blade draws 15-20% more current.

tinywhoop_class.summary: 65mm and 75mm 1S builds require a 1S whoop AIO
tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: 1S whoops need a 1S AIO board. This has the flight controller and motor drivers built in.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: Whoop AIO integrates FC+4-in-1 ESC, sometimes VTX/RX. 1S AIO cannot handle 2S voltage. Do not substitute 20x20 stack.

tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: 2S whoops need a 2S-rated AIO. Using a 1S board on 2S voltage will burn the electronics.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: 2S whoop AIO handles 7.4-8.4V input. 1S AIO max is 4.35V. Substituting a 20x20 stack is wrong form factor.

tinywhoop_class.summary: 0702 motors above 26000KV are race-tuned, not beginner-friendly
tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: Very high KV motors (26000+) are twitchy and hard to control indoors. 19000-22000KV is a better starting point.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: 0702 >26000KV optimised for race, not learning. Motor output limit to 80-85% recommended if running high-KV on 1S indoor.

tinywhoop_class.summary: Motor KV must match cell count
tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: Motor KV needs to match your battery. High-KV motors on too many cells means too much power and burned props.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: 1S targets: 19000-26000KV. 2S targets: 9000-15000KV depending on stator. Running 25000KV on 2S exceeds prop and ESC limits.

tinywhoop_class.summary: Whoop canopy must match camera width class
tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: Whoop canopies are sized for nano cameras (14mm wide). Micro cameras (19mm) won't fit without modification.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: Standard whoop canopy aperture is 14mm for nano class. Micro (19mm) requires canopy swap or trimming. DJI O3/O4 physically cannot fit sub-75mm.

tinywhoop_class.summary: Indoor whoops should target sub-25g AUW
tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: Keeping your whoop under 25g total means you avoid registration requirements in most countries.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: Sub-25g AUW avoids UK CAA and EU EASA registration. 65mm 1S builds typically land 20-24g with battery. Check local rules.

tinywhoop_class.summary: Battery capacity sweet spots by frame size
tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: 65mm: 300-450mAh batteries. 75mm 1S: 450-550mAh. 75mm 2S: 300-450mAh. Aim for 2-4 minute flights per pack.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: Capacity vs weight tradeoff. 65mm: 300mAh (8g) optimal, 450mAh (11g) max practical. 75mm 1S: 450-550mAh. 75mm 2S: 300-450mAh.

tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: Whoop motors use 1mm shaft with press-fit props. If a motor says 'M2 mounting' or 'T-mount' it's not a whoop motor.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: 0702/0802 standard: 1.0mm shaft, press-fit prop. 1102+ may use 1.5mm shaft. T-mount is incompatible with whoop prop hubs.

tinywhoop_class.summary: BT2.0 connector outperforms PH2.0 for whoop batteries
tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: BT2.0 battery connectors give more power and longer flights than the older PH2.0 connector. Pick one and stick with it, your batteries and charger need to match.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: BT2.0: significantly lower resistance than PH2.0, measurably more flight time and punch. Commit early, connector ecosystem lock-in affects batteries and charger. Adapter cables are fine for charging only, never fly with adapters.

tinywhoop_class.summary: 300mAh square cell is the current 65mm battery standard
tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: For 65mm whoops, 300mAh square cell batteries give 3+ minutes of flight. They're the current gold standard across all brands.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: 1S 300mAh 75C HV square cells (BT2.0): 3+ min sag-free. Brands (WeBleedFPV, TinyWhoop, Tattu, NewBeeDrone) believed to use the same cells. 450mAh for 75mm builds. Always charge to HV (4.35V) for full performance.

tinywhoop_class.summary: Bi-blade props can outperform tri-blade on whoops
tinywhoop_class.message_beginner: Two-blade props on whoops can actually beat three-blade in thrust, efficiency, and response. Try Gemfan 1210-2 for freestyle.
tinywhoop_class.message_advanced: Gemfan 1210-2 bi-blade outperforms most tri/quad-blade in response time, efficiency, and thrust on 65mm. 1208-3 tri-blade preferred for racing (extra grip). On 75mm, Gemfan 1610-2 bi-blade for efficiency, 1608-3 tri-blade for grip on 1002 motors.

receiver_protocol.summary: ELRS is recommended for all new builds
receiver_protocol.message_beginner: ELRS (ExpressLRS) is the community standard. Open-source, low latency, cheap, and widely supported.
receiver_protocol.message_advanced: ELRS: open-source, sub-5ms latency at 500Hz, 2.4GHz for whoops/micros, 900MHz for long range. Community standard since 2023.

receiver_protocol.summary: SPI ELRS is soldered to AIO, not replaceable
receiver_protocol.message_beginner: SPI ELRS is built into the board. It's lighter and cheaper but you can't swap it for a different protocol.
receiver_protocol.message_advanced: SPI ELRS uses SPI bus instead of UART. Saves weight (~0.5g) and a UART, but locks the board to ELRS. Cannot be replaced without changing AIO.

receiver_protocol.summary: ELRS major version mismatch breaks binding
receiver_protocol.message_beginner: Your radio and receiver must be on the same ELRS major version (both v3 or both v4). If they don't match, they won't bind.
receiver_protocol.message_advanced: ELRS v3.x TX will not bind to v2.x RX and vice versa. Most common beginner binding failure. Flash both to same major version.

receiver_protocol.summary: Start at 250Hz packet rate for beginners
receiver_protocol.message_beginner: 250Hz packet rate is plenty for learning. Higher rates (500Hz) are for racing where milliseconds matter.
receiver_protocol.message_advanced: 250Hz: good range, low latency. 500Hz: racing optimised, reduced range. 150Hz: long-range cruising. Match TX and RX packet rate.

receiver_protocol.summary: Crossfire adds weight, avoid for whoops
receiver_protocol.message_beginner: Crossfire/Tracer receivers are heavier than ELRS. On tiny whoops, every gram matters. ELRS is lighter and cheaper.
receiver_protocol.message_advanced: TBS Nano RX: ~1.5g vs ELRS EP1: ~0.5g. On sub-25g builds, 1g is 4-5% of AUW. ELRS also has better community support.

receiver_protocol.summary: Serial ELRS receiver needs a free UART
receiver_protocol.message_beginner: An external ELRS receiver needs a spare connection port (UART) on your flight controller. Most whoop boards only have 1-2.
receiver_protocol.message_advanced: Serial ELRS requires one free UART. Most whoop AIOs have 2 UARTs total. If VTX smart audio uses one, only one remains for RX.

receiver_protocol.summary: FrSky D8/D16 is legacy, avoid for new builds
receiver_protocol.message_beginner: FrSky is an older protocol. The FPV community has moved to ELRS. New builds should use ELRS.
receiver_protocol.message_advanced: FrSky D8/D16/ACCESS ecosystem is fragmented and declining. ELRS is open-source, cheaper, lower latency. Converting FrSky to ELRS is a common upgrade path.

video_system.summary: Analog video is standard for tinywhoop class
video_system.message_beginner: Analog video is the standard for tiny whoops. It's lighter, cheaper, and has lower latency than digital.
video_system.message_advanced: Analog: sub-1ms latency, ~2g VTX weight. Digital options exist for 75mm+ but add significant weight and cost.

video_system.summary: DJI O3 does not fit whoops under 3 inches
video_system.message_beginner: The DJI O3 camera is too big and heavy for whoops. You need at least a 3-inch frame to fit it.
video_system.message_advanced: DJI O3 Air Unit: 12.5g, 30.5x30.5mm mounting. Minimum viable platform is 3-inch cinewhoop. Does not fit any whoop AIO or 2-inch frame.

video_system.summary: HDZero Whoop Lite is the only sub-3-inch digital option
video_system.message_beginner: HDZero Whoop Lite is the only digital video system light enough for whoops. It needs HDZero goggles though.
video_system.message_advanced: HDZero Whoop Lite: ~2g, fits on whoop AIO. Only digital option for 65-75mm class. Requires HDZero-compatible goggles (not DJI, not analog).

video_system.message_beginner: Your goggles must match your video system. Analog goggles can't see DJI, DJI goggles can't see analog.
video_system.message_advanced: No cross-compatibility: analog requires analog RX module, DJI requires DJI goggles, HDZero requires HDZero goggles, Walksnail requires Walksnail goggles.

video_system.message_beginner: Use 25mW power for flying indoors. Higher power doesn't help in a living room and can interfere with other pilots.
video_system.message_advanced: 25mW for indoor/proximity. 200mW+ for outdoor range. Race events mandate 25mW pit mode. Higher power causes multipath interference indoors.

video_system.summary: Walksnail Avatar Nano fits 75mm+ with weight penalty
video_system.message_beginner: Walksnail Avatar Nano can fit on 75mm whoops but adds 5-7g. Only practical on 2S builds with enough power to carry the weight.
video_system.message_advanced: Walksnail Avatar Nano: ~5-7g, fits 75mm+ frames. Feasible on 75mm 2S with 1102 motors. Weight penalty significant on 1S builds.

video_system.summary: Never power a VTX without an antenna connected
video_system.message_beginner: Never plug in a battery with your video transmitter connected but no antenna attached. It will burn out the VTX instantly.
video_system.message_advanced: Operating a VTX without a matched load (antenna) causes reflected RF energy that destroys the output amplifier. This applies to all VTX types: analog, DJI, HDZero, Walksnail. Even a few seconds can cause permanent damage.

video_system.summary: Antenna polarity must match between quad and goggles
video_system.message_beginner: Your antenna on the drone and the antenna on your goggles must be the same type: both RHCP or both LHCP. Mismatched antennas cause terrible video.
video_system.message_advanced: RHCP/LHCP cross-polarisation causes ~20dB signal loss, equivalent to reducing VTX power by 100x. RHCP is the community default. Match polarity on all antennas in the system. Lumenier colour codes: white=LHCP, black=RHCP.

video_system.summary: HDZero goggles are the only triple-system option
video_system.message_beginner: HDZero goggles can receive analog, HDZero, and Walksnail video. They're the only goggles that work with all three systems.
video_system.message_advanced: HDZero goggles: native HDZero at 90fps/14ms latency. Analog via module bay with best-in-class deinterlacing. Walksnail via HDMI at 100fps (lowest Walksnail latency available). The only single-goggle solution for all analog/digital systems.

battery_connector.summary: BT2.0 has lower resistance than PH2.0
battery_connector.message_beginner: BT2.0 connectors deliver more power than PH2.0. If you're buying new batteries, BT2.0 is the better choice.
battery_connector.message_advanced: BT2.0: ~4 milliohm contact resistance vs PH2.0: 10-20 milliohm. On 1S, this is 5-10% power delivery difference. BT2.0 is newer standard.

battery_connector.summary: BT2.0 and PH2.0 are not cross-compatible
battery_connector.message_beginner: BT2.0 and PH2.0 batteries use different connectors. They don't plug into each other. Check which your board uses.
battery_connector.message_advanced: BT2.0 and PH2.0 have different pin spacing and physical form factor. No direct cross-compatibility. Adapters exist but add resistance, defeating the purpose.

battery_connector.summary: Battery capacity must match frame size
battery_connector.message_beginner: Bigger batteries are heavier. A 550mAh battery on a 65mm whoop makes it sluggish and hard to fly.
battery_connector.message_advanced: 65mm 1S: max practical 450mAh (~11g). Above that, thrust-to-weight ratio drops below acceptable. 300mAh (8-9g) is optimal for indoor.

battery_connector.message_beginner: Always storage charge your batteries (3.8V) if you won't fly within a day. Leaving them fully charged ruins them within weeks.
battery_connector.message_advanced: Full charge (4.2V/cell) degrades capacity within 1-2 weeks. Full depletion (<3.3V/cell) can permanently damage cells. Storage voltage: 3.8V/cell.

battery_connector.message_beginner: Puffed batteries are a fire risk. Don't sell or swap them. Dispose of them safely at a recycling point.
battery_connector.message_advanced: Puffed LiPo indicates internal gas generation from cell damage. Fire risk during charging. Should not enter swap listings. Flag for safe disposal.

motor_prop_sizing.message_beginner: Motor size numbers tell you what they're for. 0702 = tiny indoor, 0802 = standard whoop, 1404 = cinewhoop, 2306 = 5-inch freestyle.
motor_prop_sizing.message_advanced: Stator volume (diameter x height) determines thrust class. 0702: 1S micro. 0802: 1S whoop. 1102: 2S whoop/2-inch. 1404: 3-inch. 1505: 3.5-inch. 2306: 5-inch.

motor_prop_sizing.summary: Prop size must match frame exactly
motor_prop_sizing.message_beginner: Props must fit your frame. Whoop ducts are a fixed diameter, so only one prop size works per frame.
motor_prop_sizing.message_advanced: Whoop ducts: fixed diameter, zero clearance tolerance. Open frames: prop must not exceed arm length. 65mm=31mm, 75mm=40mm, 3-inch=3-inch, 5-inch=5-inch.

motor_prop_sizing.summary: Higher blade count means more thrust but more current
motor_prop_sizing.message_beginner: More prop blades = more grip and power, but your batteries drain faster. Tri-blade is the standard balance.
motor_prop_sizing.message_advanced: Tri-blade is community standard. Quad-blade adds 15-20% current draw for ~10% more thrust. Bi-blade: efficient but less responsive.

motor_prop_sizing.summary: Prop shaft diameter must match motor shaft
motor_prop_sizing.message_beginner: Props must fit your motor shaft. Whoop motors use 1mm shaft. Bigger motors use 1.5mm or 5mm (M5).
motor_prop_sizing.message_advanced: Shaft standards: 0702/0802: 1.0mm press-fit. 1102/1203: 1.5mm. 1404+: 1.5mm or M5. T-mount props won't fit press-fit motors.

bnf_upgrade_pathway.summary: BNF means Bind-and-Fly, no radio or goggles included
bnf_upgrade_pathway.message_beginner: BNF (Bind-and-Fly) means you get the drone but need your own radio and goggles. Check the receiver protocol before buying.
bnf_upgrade_pathway.message_advanced: BNF: quad only. Verify receiver protocol matches your TX (ELRS, FrSky, etc). Common beginner mistake: buying wrong protocol BNF.

bnf_upgrade_pathway.summary: RTF kits include everything to start flying
bnf_upgrade_pathway.message_beginner: RTF (Ready to Fly) kits include the drone, radio, goggles, batteries, and charger. Everything you need in one box.
bnf_upgrade_pathway.message_advanced: RTF kits (Cetus X, Tinyhawk III RTF): complete ecosystem. Entry-level components but functional. Ecosystem lock to manufacturer radio/goggles.

bnf_upgrade_pathway.summary: Common upgrade path: radio first, then goggles
bnf_upgrade_pathway.message_beginner: If you started with an RTF kit, upgrading the radio first gives the biggest improvement. RadioMaster Pocket or Zorro are popular choices.
bnf_upgrade_pathway.message_advanced: Upgrade priority: 1) Radio (RadioMaster Pocket/Zorro/Boxer with ELRS). 2) Goggles. 3) Additional quads. Radio upgrade unlocks all ELRS BNF builds.

bnf_upgrade_pathway.summary: Swapping receiver protocol requires soldering on serial, impossible on SPI
bnf_upgrade_pathway.message_beginner: Changing the receiver protocol on a whoop means soldering a new receiver chip. If it's built into the board (SPI), it can't be changed at all.
bnf_upgrade_pathway.message_advanced: Serial RX swap: desolder old, solder new to same UART pads. SPI RX: hardware bus on AIO PCB, not replaceable. SPI lock = protocol lock for life of that board.

bnf_upgrade_pathway.summary: Stock BNF holds swap value better than modified builds
bnf_upgrade_pathway.message_beginner: Unmodified BNF drones are easier to sell or swap because buyers know exactly what they're getting.
bnf_upgrade_pathway.message_advanced: Stock BNF: model name acts as strong evidence bundle for all components. Modified builds reduce model-match confidence and require more disclosure.

charger_storage.summary: 1S charger must match battery connector family
charger_storage.message_beginner: BT2.0 charger boards only charge BT2.0 batteries. PH2.0 boards only charge PH2.0. Make sure they match.
charger_storage.message_advanced: 1S parallel charging boards are connector-specific. BT2.0 and PH2.0 are physically incompatible. Verify board matches battery connector.

charger_storage.summary: Storage mode is mandatory for batteries not used within 24 hours
charger_storage.message_beginner: Always put batteries in storage mode if you won't fly tomorrow. It's the single biggest thing you can do to make them last.
charger_storage.message_advanced: Storage charge (3.8V/cell) is the primary factor in LiPo longevity. Full charge stored >48hrs measurably degrades capacity. Number one cause of premature 1S battery death.

charger_storage.message_beginner: Charge your batteries gently. A 300mAh battery should charge at 0.3A or less for longest life.
charger_storage.message_advanced: 1C charge rate: 300mAh = 0.3A, 450mAh = 0.45A. Higher rates reduce cycle life. Some charger boards default to aggressive rates.

charger_storage.message_beginner: Only parallel charge batteries of the same type and similar voltage. Mixing sizes or charge levels is a fire risk.
charger_storage.message_advanced: Parallel charging: same capacity, same cell count, voltage within 0.1V of each other. Never mix 300mAh and 550mAh. Never mix fresh and degraded cells.

cinewhoop_micro.summary: 3-inch is the smallest viable DJI platform
cinewhoop_micro.message_beginner: 3-inch is the smallest frame that can carry a DJI camera. Use 1404 motors on 4S with prop guards.
cinewhoop_micro.message_advanced: 3-inch cinewhoop: 1404-1505 motors, 4S, ducted props, 150-200g AUW. DJI O3 Air Unit fits 20x20 mount. Minimum viable DJI platform.

cinewhoop_micro.message_beginner: 2-inch builds sit between whoops and cinewhoops. More powerful than a whoop but too small for DJI video.
cinewhoop_micro.message_advanced: 2-inch: 1102-1203 motors, 2S-3S, analog or HDZero only. Too small for DJI O3. Bridge class between whoop and cinewhoop.

cinewhoop_micro.message_beginner: 3.5-inch frames can carry heavier cameras than 3-inch. Better for full action cameras if you need the footage quality.
cinewhoop_micro.message_advanced: 3.5-inch: 1505-2004 motors, 4S-6S, supports naked GoPro or full action camera. Better payload margin than 3-inch for camera builds.

cinewhoop_micro.summary: Naked GoPro for lightweight camera on 3-inch
cinewhoop_micro.message_beginner: A 'naked GoPro' is a stripped-down action camera that weighs about 30g instead of 120g. Popular for cinewhoops.
cinewhoop_micro.message_advanced: Naked GoPro: ~30g vs 120g full. SMO4K: ~30g purpose-built. For 3-inch builds, full-size camera is marginal. 3.5-inch handles full-size better.

cinewhoop_micro.message_beginner: 5-inch freestyle quads are open-frame, no prop guards, and much more powerful. They're not cinewhoops. Different flying style entirely.
cinewhoop_micro.message_advanced: 5-inch freestyle: 2204-2306 motors, 4S-6S, open frame, no ducts. Not safe for proximity/indoor. Cinewhoop = ducted props + camera focus.

five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: 5-inch builds use 22xx or 23xx motors. Smaller motors can't handle 5-inch props, larger motors are overkill and add weight.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: 5-inch standard: 2205-2306 stators. 2207 is the community workhorse. 2406 for heavy camera builds. Anything below 2204 lacks torque for 5-inch props.

five_inch_freestyle.summary: 5-inch builds need a 30x30 or 20x20 stack, not an AIO
five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: 5-inch quads use a separate flight controller and ESC (called a stack), not a whoop AIO board. The current draw is too high for AIO boards.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: 5-inch draws 80-120A burst. Whoop AIOs max at 12-25A. 30x30 is standard mount, 20x20 for lightweight builds. 4-in-1 ESC + separate FC.

five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: 4S batteries need higher KV motors (2400KV). 6S batteries need lower KV (1750-1950KV). Wrong KV for your battery means either no power or blown motors.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: RPM = KV x voltage. 6S freestyle sweet spot: 1800-1955KV (Bardwell recommends 1855KV as all-rounder). 4S: 2400KV. 1700KV on 6S gives longer flight time but less punch. 1950KV+ on 6S is more aggressive.

five_inch_freestyle.summary: XT60 connector standard for 4S and above
five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: 5-inch and larger builds use XT60 connectors. XT30 can't handle the current draw safely.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: XT60 rated 60A continuous, 120A burst. XT30 rated 30A/60A. 5-inch 6S draws 80-120A burst. XT30 will overheat and melt under freestyle loads.

five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: 5040 or 5051 tri-blade props are the standard for freestyle. Higher pitch (51) gives more speed, lower pitch (40) is smoother.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: 5040: balanced efficiency and thrust. 5051: aggressive pitch for speed/punch. 5045: middle ground. Bi-blade for efficiency, quad-blade for grip in turns.

five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: If you're flying 5-inch beyond visual line of sight, add a GPS module. It enables return-to-home if you lose signal.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: GPS rescue mode in Betaflight/iNav. ~5g weight penalty. Essential for LR. BN-880 or M10 GPS modules. Requires a free UART.

five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: Mounting a GoPro or action camera adds about 120-150g. Your quad will feel heavier and less agile. Tune accordingly.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: Full GoPro: 120-150g. Naked GoPro: 25-35g. DJI Action 2: 56g. Weight affects PID tune, propwash handling, and flight time. Re-tune after adding camera.

five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: 5-inch frames should have arms at least 4mm thick. Thinner arms break on crashes.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: 4mm carbon arms standard for freestyle. 5mm for racing (stiffer, heavier). 3mm only viable for ultralight builds with careful flying. True-X geometry for freestyle, stretched-X for racing.

five_inch_freestyle.summary: Typical 5-inch freestyle AUW is 600-750g with battery
five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: A 5-inch freestyle build usually weighs 600-750g with battery. Under 600g is ultralight, over 800g is heavy and will fly sluggish.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: 5-inch freestyle AUW target: 650-700g with 1300mAh 6S. Sub-600g ultralight possible with analog. Over 750g with action cam is normal. 670g is a current sweet spot for aggressive freestyle.

five_inch_freestyle.summary: Props out gaining adoption for freestyle over props in
five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: Motor direction (props in vs props out) is a personal preference. Props out is becoming more popular for freestyle because it reduces prop entanglement in trees.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: Props in: slightly more centre authority, traditional default. Props out: better dirty air recovery, less likely to wrap branches. Most firmware defaults to props out now. Performance difference is minimal, it's mostly about handling feel.

five_inch_freestyle.summary: 6S 1100-1400mAh is the freestyle battery sweet spot
five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: For 5-inch freestyle, a 6S battery between 1100-1400mAh gives the best balance of flight time and weight. 1300mAh is the most popular choice.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: 6S freestyle battery: 1100-1400mAh, 180-220g, minimum 100C. 1300mAh 6S at ~200g is the community standard. Higher capacity (1550mAh) adds weight but suits cinematic/cruising. Below 1100mAh noticeably shorter flights.

five_inch_freestyle.summary: Prop pitch 3.5-3.7 inch for responsive freestyle
five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: For freestyle, 5-inch props with 3.5-3.7 inch pitch give the best throttle response. Lower pitch (3.5) is smoother, higher pitch (3.7+) is more aggressive.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: Gemfan Hurricane 51433 (3.5" pitch): smooth, efficient. 51466 (3.6"): balanced. 51477 (3.7"): aggressive freestyle. HQ Ethix S3/S5: lightweight, responsive on sub-650g builds. Gemfan Sbang 4934: snappy for sbang style. 51499 (4.2"): racing oriented.

five_inch_freestyle.summary: Lightweight builds under 650g benefit from lighter props
five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: If your 5-inch build is under 650g, use lighter props like the HQ Ethix series. They give better throttle feel on light builds.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: HQ Ethix S3/S5: optimised for 500-650g AUW. On heavier builds (700g+ with action cam), switch to Gemfan Hurricane for more thrust. Prop choice should match build weight, not just frame size.

five_inch_freestyle.summary: TBS Lucid H7 is a current go-to FC for 5-inch builds
five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: The TBS Lucid H7 flight controller is a popular choice for 5-inch builds. It has dual gyros, 7 UARTs, and supports up to 8S batteries.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: TBS Lucid H7: dual gyros, baro, 13 motor outputs, 7 UARTs, dual camera switcher, 8S, SD card blackbox. Betaflight/iNav/Ardupilot compatible. Bardwell's current go-to FC. Pair with TBS Lucid 60A ESC for a complete stack.

five_inch_freestyle.message_beginner: The ImpulseRC Apex was a legendary freestyle frame but the company has shut down. Spare parts are hard to find. Look at alternatives for new builds.
five_inch_freestyle.message_advanced: ImpulseRC discontinued. Apex spare parts are unobtanium. Current alternatives: Vannystyle, Badger, Five33 Switchback, TBS Source One (open source). If you find an Apex, hold onto it.

long_range.message_beginner: 7-inch long-range builds need larger motors than 5-inch. 2806 or 2807 are the standard sizes for 7-inch props.
long_range.message_advanced: 7-inch: 2806.5-2807 for efficiency at cruise. 3010/3110 for cinematic payload. Lower KV than 5-inch: 1100-1500KV on 6S.

long_range.message_beginner: For long-range flying, use 915MHz ELRS instead of 2.4GHz. Lower frequency travels much further.
long_range.message_advanced: 915MHz ELRS: 30-50km+ range at 100mW. 2.4GHz ELRS: 5-15km practical range. 915MHz has better obstacle penetration and diffraction. Use 150Hz or 50Hz packet rates for maximum range.

long_range.summary: GPS and return-to-home mandatory for long range
long_range.message_beginner: Long-range builds must have GPS for return-to-home. Without it, a signal loss means a lost quad.
long_range.message_advanced: GPS rescue in Betaflight or iNav. M10 GPS recommended for fast fix. Set failsafe to GPS rescue, not drop. Test RTH before flying distance.

long_range.summary: Battery capacity vs weight for long-range endurance
long_range.message_beginner: Bigger batteries fly longer but add weight. For 7-inch long range, 1300-1800mAh 6S is the sweet spot. Aim for 20-40 minute flights.
long_range.message_advanced: 7-inch 6S: 1300mAh for agility, 1800mAh for endurance. Li-ion 18650 packs offer 2x flight time at cost of burst current. Samsung 30Q 4S2P for maximum range.

long_range.message_beginner: 10-inch builds are heavy and need large motors. 2812 or bigger is required to spin 10-inch props effectively.
long_range.message_advanced: 10-inch cinematic/LR: 2812-3120 stators at 300-600KV on 6S-8S. These are slow-spinning, high-torque platforms for cinema cameras or extreme endurance.

digital_video_ecosystem.summary: DJI O4 requires DJI Goggles 3 or N3
digital_video_ecosystem.message_beginner: DJI O4 only works with DJI Goggles 3 or Goggles N3. Older DJI goggles (V1, V2, Integra) do not support O4.
digital_video_ecosystem.message_advanced: DJI O4 uses a new protocol incompatible with O3-era goggles. Goggles 3 support both O3 and O4. Goggles 2/V2/Integra only support O3.

digital_video_ecosystem.summary: Walksnail goggles are not compatible with DJI
digital_video_ecosystem.message_beginner: Walksnail and DJI are completely separate systems. Walksnail goggles only work with Walksnail VTX, and DJI goggles only work with DJI air units.
digital_video_ecosystem.message_advanced: Despite both being 'digital HD', Walksnail Avatar and DJI use incompatible protocols. No cross-compatibility. Choosing one locks your goggle and VTX ecosystem.

digital_video_ecosystem.summary: HDZero Race VTX needs 20x20 or 25x25 mount
digital_video_ecosystem.message_beginner: The HDZero Race and Freestyle VTX boards are 20x20 or 25x25 mount. Check your frame supports this stack size.
digital_video_ecosystem.message_advanced: HDZero Race V3: 25.5x25.5mm, ~6g. Fits 20x20 and 25x25 frames. HDZero Freestyle V3: same mount. Whoop Lite: 16x16mm for sub-3-inch.

digital_video_ecosystem.message_beginner: The full Walksnail Avatar VTX is too heavy for anything smaller than a 3-inch frame. Use the Avatar Nano for smaller builds.
digital_video_ecosystem.message_advanced: Walksnail Avatar VTX: ~10g + camera ~8g. Total ~18g digital system. Minimum viable on 3-inch 4S. Avatar Nano/Mini for 75mm-2.5-inch class at ~7g total.

digital_video_ecosystem.summary: DJI O4 cameras are fragile and expensive to replace
digital_video_ecosystem.message_beginner: DJI O4 cameras break easily on crashes and cost $300-400 to replace. Protect them with a TPU mount and avoid bando bashing until you're confident.
digital_video_ecosystem.message_advanced: O4 camera module $300-400 USD, often out of stock. Frame-specific TPU mounts and conformal coating help. Some pilots mod the camera for durability. Stock availability is inconsistent, buy spares when you can find them.

digital_video_ecosystem.summary: Analog video is seeing renewed interest due to DJI cost
digital_video_ecosystem.message_beginner: Analog video is cheaper and easier to repair than DJI. Some experienced pilots are going back to analog because replacing digital gear after crashes gets expensive.
digital_video_ecosystem.message_advanced: DJI O4 ecosystem cost: goggles $500+, VTX $80+, camera $300+. Analog full setup under $100. For crash-heavy flying styles (bando, racing), analog TCO is significantly lower. Analog latency is sub-1ms vs DJI ~28ms.
